files.title: Title Page, Copyright, and Front Matter
files.summary: Covers the title page, author list, copyright information, license details, publisher information, and source code availability for the book 'Data Parallel C++: Programming Accelerated Systems Using C++ and SYCL, Second Edition'.

files.title: Table of Contents
files.summary: The complete table of contents listing all chapters (1-21), epilogue, and index, along with their respective starting page numbers.

files.title: About the Authors
files.summary: Biographical sketches of the authors: James Reinders, Ben Ashbaugh, James Brodman, Michael Kinsner, John Pennycook, and Xinmin Tian, detailing their roles at Intel and expertise in parallel computing.

files.title: Preface and Introduction to Second Edition
files.summary: Covers the preface for the second edition, including the rationale for using SYCL with C++, the structure of the book, the foreword by Erik Lindahl, and acknowledgments.

files.title: Introduction to SYCL and Parallel Computing Concepts
files.summary: Introduces the core concepts of accelerated and heterogeneous computing, the role of SYCL, queues, kernels, asynchronous execution, race conditions, deadlocks, and the importance of thinking in parallel. It also covers the distinction between functional and performance portability and sets the stage for understanding concurrency.
files.header: Introduction to SYCL and Parallel Computing

files.title: C++ Lambda Expressions and Function Objects
files.summary: Continuation of the previous chunk, covering C++ lambda expression syntax, capture lists, parameters, return types, and the analogy between lambdas and function objects (functors), including code examples and output.
files.header: C++ Lambda Expressions and Function Objects

files.title: Portability and Concurrency Concepts
files.summary: Discusses functional and performance portability in C++ with SYCL, distinguishing between the two concepts. It then defines concurrency versus parallelism, explaining the differences, hardware limitations, and potential pitfalls like deadlock. Ends with the chapter summary and license information.

files.title: Where Code Executes: Single-Source and Host/Device Code
files.summary: Introduction to Chapter 2, explaining that code can execute on various compute resources in a heterogeneous system. It defines single-source programs, host code (standard C++ with SYCL constructs), and device code (asynchronous, restricted, accelerator-focused). It introduces the queue as the mechanism for submitting work.
files.header: Where Code Executes: Single-Source and Host/Device Code

files.title: Device Selection Methods: Default, CPU, and GPU
files.summary: Details five methods for choosing devices in SYCL. Method 1 covers automatic device selection via default constructors. Method 2 focuses on using CPU devices for debugging and development. Method 3 explains targeting GPUs and other accelerators using built-in selectors like gpu_selector_v. It includes code examples, queue constructor definitions, and error handling when selection fails.
files.header: Device Selection Methods: Default, CPU, and GPU

files.title: Advanced Device Selection: Multiple Devices and Custom Selectors
files.summary: Covers methods for selecting specific devices in SYCL, including using multiple queues bound to different devices (GPU/FPGA) and creating custom device selectors based on device aspects or custom scoring logic.
files.header: Advanced Device Selection: Multiple Devices and Custom Selectors

files.title: Creating Work on a Device: Task Graphs and Actions
files.summary: Explains the SYCL execution model, including the task graph concept, distinguishing between host code and device code, the handler and queue classes for submitting work, specific actions like parallel_for and host_task, and the asynchronous nature of kernel execution.
files.header: Creating Work on a Device: Task Graphs and Actions

files.title: Data Management: USM and Buffers
files.summary: Covers the Data Management chapter content, including the problem of multiple memories, explicit vs implicit data movement, Unified Shared Memory (USM) allocation types and examples, Buffer creation and access modes, and task ordering using in-order/out-of-order queues, events, and implicit dependencies via accessors.

files.title: Choosing a Data Management Strategy and Handler Reference
files.summary: Provides guidelines for selecting data management strategies (explicit/implicit, buffers/USM), details the key members of the SYCL handler class with code examples, and includes the chapter summary and licensing information.
files.header: Data Management: Strategy Selection and Handler Reference

files.title: Expressing Parallelism: Kernels vs Loops
files.summary: Introduction to expressing parallelism in SYCL, contrasting parallel loops with parallel kernels, and explaining multidimensional indexing conventions.

files.title: Expressing Parallelism: Basic Data-Parallel Kernels
files.summary: Overview of language features, lambda expressions, and the three kernel forms. Detailed explanation of basic data-parallel kernels, including the range, id, and item classes.

files.title: Expressing Parallelism: Explicit ND-Range Kernels
files.summary: Explanation of explicit ND-range kernels, covering work-items, work-groups, sub-groups, and the nd_range, nd_item, group, and sub_group classes.

files.title: Expressing Parallelism: Mapping and Kernel Selection
files.summary: Covers one-to-one and many-to-one mapping strategies for SYCL kernels, including code examples for work distribution, and provides guidance on choosing the appropriate kernel form based on experience and requirements. Includes the chapter summary and license information.
files.header: Expressing Parallelism: Mapping and Kernel Selection

files.title: Error Handling
files.summary: Introduces synchronous and asynchronous error handling in SYCL. Explains the separation between host execution and task graphs, details how to handle exceptions for both error types, and discusses device-side error signaling limitations. Includes the chapter summary and license information.

files.title: Unified Shared Memory
files.summary: Covers the Unified Shared Memory (USM) model, including allocation types (device, host, shared), allocation interfaces (C-style, C++-style, allocators), deallocation, data initialization, explicit and implicit data movement, migration behaviors, fine-grained control via prefetch/mem_advise, and device/pointer queries.

files.title: Buffers
files.summary: Introduction to the buffer abstraction in SYCL, including its definition, creation methods (default, host pointer, containers, sub-buffers), and special properties (use_host_ptr, use_mutex, context_bound).

files.title: Accessors
files.summary: Detailed explanation of accessor objects used to access buffer data, including template parameters, access modes, targets, placeholder vs. regular accessors, creation examples, deduction tags, and accessor capabilities.

files.title: Scheduling Kernels and Data Movement
files.summary: This file completes the previous chunk's section on scheduling kernels and data movement, covering the summary of the chapter and the transition to the next topic.

files.title: Communication and Synchronization
files.summary: This file covers Chapter 9, which discusses work-groups, work-items, barriers, local memory, sub-groups, collective functions, and group algorithms for efficient communication and synchronization in SYCL.

files.title: Defining Kernels
files.summary: Covers the three ways to define SYCL kernels: lambda expressions, named function objects (functors), and interoperability with other APIs. Includes details on lambda syntax, capture rules, kernel identification, functor structure, device copyability, attributes, and kernel bundle management for compilation and introspection.

files.title: Vectors and Math Arrays
files.summary: Covers the concepts of vector types in SYCL, distinguishing between convenience types (marray) and hardware-mapped types (vec/SIMD). Includes details on marray usage, vec load/store operations, swizzle operations, and how these types execute on hardware (SPMD vs. SIMD interpretations).

files.title: Device Information and Kernel Specialization
files.summary: This section covers the introduction to device information and kernel specialization in SYCL. It discusses how to query device capabilities (such as checking for GPU presence, enumerating devices, and using aspects like fp16/fp64 support) to make programs portable and performant. It introduces custom device selectors, the get_info<> interface for detailed device and kernel queries, and distinguishes between correctness-based device constraints (like max work group size) and tuning parameters (like preferred work group size). The section concludes by explaining how to use SYCL attributes (like device_has and reqd_work_group_size) to specialize kernels for specific device features.

files.title: Practical Tips: Compilation, Debugging, and Profiling
files.summary: Continuation of the previous chunk covering Chapter 13 Practical Tips, including device information attributes, compilation models, context management, and debugging techniques.
files.header: Practical Tips: Compilation, Debugging, and Profiling

files.title: Practical Tips: Data Access, Translation Units, and Lambda Naming
files.summary: Covers common bugs related to buffer/host allocation lifetime, host accessor usage, multiple translation units with SYCL_EXTERNAL, performance implications, and named lambdas for debugging and tools.
files.header: Practical Tips: Data Access, Translation Units, and Lambda Naming

files.title: Common Parallel Patterns: Overview, Map, Stencil, Reduction, Scan, Pack/Unpack
files.summary: Introduces Chapter 14 on common parallel patterns, detailing Map, Stencil, Reduction, Scan, Pack, and Unpack patterns, their use cases, device affinities, and implementation considerations.
files.header: Common Parallel Patterns: Overview, Map, Stencil, Reduction, Scan, Pack/Unpack

files.title: Common Parallel Patterns: SYCL Reduction Library and Implementation
files.summary: Continuation of the previous chunk covering the SYCL Reduction Library, the reduction and reducer classes, and user-defined reductions.
files.header: Common Parallel Patterns: Overview, Map, Stencil, Reduction, Scan, Pack/Unpack

files.title: Common Parallel Patterns: Group Algorithms and Direct Programming
files.summary: Covers Group Algorithms (joint and group variants), mapping C++ algorithms to SYCL, and the transition to Direct Programming patterns like Map, Stencil, Reduction, and Scan.
files.header: Common Parallel Patterns: Group Algorithms and Direct Programming

files.title: Common Parallel Patterns: Pack/Unpack and Summary
files.summary: Covers Pack and Unpack patterns (gather/scatter) with examples, followed by the chapter summary and for more information section.
files.header: Common Parallel Patterns: Pack/Unpack and Summary

files.title: Programming for GPUs: Overview and Building Blocks
files.summary: Introduces the GPU programming chapter, covering performance caveats, how GPUs work, and GPU building blocks (execution resources, fixed functions, caches/memory).
files.header: Programming for GPUs: Overview and Building Blocks

files.title: Programming for GPUs: Simpler Processors and Matrix Multiplication Intro
files.summary: Continuation of the previous chunk. Covers the reminder about matrix multiplication, the importance of using vendor libraries, and the initial single-task kernel example that performs poorly on GPUs.
files.header: Programming for GPUs: Simpler Processors and Matrix Multiplication Intro

files.title: Expressing Parallelism in GPU Kernels
files.summary: Covers converting loops to parallel_for to utilize GPU processors, choosing parallelization dimensions, and the concept of SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) execution where multiple data elements share control logic.

files.title: SIMD Efficiency and Latency Hiding
files.summary: Explains predication and masking for divergent control flow, SIMD efficiency metrics, the importance of work-group sizes relative to SIMD width, and using multiple instruction streams to hide memory latency.

files.title: Offloading Kernels to GPUs
files.summary: Details the software stack involved in offloading (SYCL Runtime, GPU Drivers, Hardware), the costs and overheads associated with offloading, and the trade-off between latency and throughput.

files.title: GPU Memory Access Best Practices
files.summary: Covers global vs. device memory transfers, coalesced memory access patterns for global memory, and optimizing local memory usage by avoiding bank conflicts. Includes comparison of access patterns.

files.title: Optimizing GPU Computation
files.summary: Discusses using sub-groups to avoid local memory, optimizing with smaller data types (16-bit vs 32-bit), using fast math functions, and leveraging specialized GPU instructions like FMA.

files.title: GPU Chapter Summary and References
files.summary: Summarizes the GPU chapter content including architecture, parallelism, offloading, and optimization. Provides references for further information and licensing details.

files.title: Programming for CPUs: Introduction and Architecture
files.summary: Introduces CPU programming for SYCL, covering multicore evolution, cc-NUMA architecture, cache coherency, and the five levels of parallelism (instruction, thread, core, socket, cluster).

files.title: CPU SIMD and STREAM Triad Example
files.summary: Introduction to the STREAM Triad workload, demonstrating the transition from serial C++ to SYCL parallel_for kernels and mapping to CPU cores.
files.header: CPU Programming: SIMD and STREAM Triad Example

files.title: Thread-Level Parallelism and Affinity on CPUs
files.summary: Discussion on exploiting thread-level parallelism, thread affinity environment variables (DPCPP_CPU_CU_AFFINITY, etc.), and scheduling strategies to optimize CPU performance.
files.header: CPU Programming: Thread-Level Parallelism and Affinity

files.title: Memory First Touch and SIMD Vectorization
files.summary: Guidelines for controlling first-touch effects, SIMD vectorization mechanics, masking costs, avoiding Array-of-Structs (AOS), and data type impacts on SIMD efficiency.
files.header: CPU Programming: Memory First Touch and SIMD Vectorization

files.title: CPU Summary and FPGA Introduction
files.summary: Summary of CPU optimization strategies and introduction to Chapter 17 on Programming for FPGAs, including performance caveats.
files.header: CPU Programming: Summary and FPGA Introduction

files.title: How to Think About FPGAs and Pipeline Parallelism
files.summary: Explanation of spatial architecture vs. ISA-based processors, pipeline parallelism, and automatic pipelining in FPGA compilers.
files.header: FPGA Spatial Architecture and Pipeline Parallelism

files.title: FPGA Use Cases and Execution
files.summary: Continuation of the previous chunk covering control flow optimization, kernel area consumption, when to use FPGAs (lots of work, custom operations, scalar data flow, loops, low latency I/O, custom memory), and running kernels on FPGAs including compilation commands, runtime device selection, and the FPGA emulator for rapid development.

files.title: Writing Kernels for FPGAs
files.summary: Covers writing kernels for FPGAs, including exposing parallelism via ND-ranges and loops, handling data dependences with loop pipelining and initiation intervals, and using pipes (FIFOs) for modular design and inter-kernel communication.

files.title: FPGA Architecture and Closing Topics
files.summary: Covers FPGA building blocks (LUTs, DSPs, memory, interfaces, routing), clock frequency considerations, resource utilization recommendations, and the chapter summary with licensing information.

files.title: Libraries: Introduction and Built-In Functions
files.summary: Introduction to the Libraries chapter, covering built-in SYCL functions, their classification, usage with the sycl:: prefix, and comparison with C++ standard library functions.

files.title: Libraries: C++ Standard Library and oneDPL
files.summary: Coverage of the C++ standard library support in SYCL devices, oneAPI DPC++ Library (oneDPL) execution policies, usage with buffers and USM, and error handling.
files.header: Libraries: C++ Standard Library and oneDPL

files.title: Memory Model and Atomics: Introduction
files.summary: Introduction to the memory model, threads of execution, data races, barriers, and atomics.

files.title: Memory Model and Atomics: Memory Ordering and Scopes
files.summary: Details on memory ordering (relaxed, acquire-release, sequential consistency), memory scopes, and querying device capabilities.
files.header: Memory Model and Atomics: Memory Ordering and Scopes

files.title: Memory Model and Atomics: Atomic Operations in SYCL
files.summary: Comparison of std::atomic and atomic_ref, atomic operations for buffers and USM, and practical examples like histograms.
files.header: Memory Model and Atomics: Atomic Operations in SYCL

files.title: Memory Model and Atomics: Device-Wide Synchronization
files.summary: Continuation of the Memory Model and Atomics chapter, covering the histogram example implementation, device-wide synchronization concepts, and the device latch implementation code.
files.header: Memory Model and Atomics: Device-Wide Synchronization

files.title: Memory Model and Atomics: Summary and References
files.summary: Completion of the device latch example, including conditions for correctness, code blocks for the latch and its usage, the chapter summary, and references for further reading on memory models.
files.header: Memory Model and Atomics: Summary and References

files.title: Backend Interoperability: Introduction and Concepts
files.summary: Start of Chapter 20, introducing backend interoperability, its purpose, mental models, and how to create SYCL objects from native backend objects (OpenCL and Level Zero) with ownership considerations.

files.title: Backend Interoperability: Extracting Backend Objects
files.summary: Methods for extracting native backend objects from SYCL objects, including using free functions (get_native) and interop handles within host tasks, with examples for OpenCL and Level Zero.

files.title: Backend Interoperability: Kernel Integration
files.summary: Interoperability for kernels, including creating kernels from API-defined objects and non-SYCL source languages (via online compilers and SPIR-V), with code examples and notes on argument passing.

files.title: Backend Interoperability Hints and Tips
files.summary: This section covers practical hints for backend interoperability, including choosing devices by backend, managing contexts safely, accessing low-level API features, and supporting other backends. It concludes the previous chapter (Chapter 20) with a summary and licensing information.

files.title: Migrating CUDA Code (continued)
files.summary: Covers design, terminology, and execution/memory model differences between CUDA and SYCL, including barriers, atomics, item classes, and contexts. Continues with porting tools, demonstrating the dpct migration of a sample CUDA application.

files.title: Future Direction of SYCL
files.summary: An epilogue speculating on the future of SYCL, including alignment with C++ standards (C++11-23), mixed SPMD/SIMD programming, address spaces, specialization mechanisms, and compile-time properties, along with references and resources.

files.title: Index
files.summary: The alphabetical index for the book, listing topics, concepts, and function/class names with their corresponding page numbers.
